title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their relationship with digital technology. For questions 43-52, choose from the people (A-D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Switching Off: Four People on Managing Screen Time

texts.A:
### Lena, 38, Marketing Consultant
[43]About two years ago I made a conscious decision to stop checking my phone for the first hour of every morning and the last hour before bed, which was harder than any fitness challenge I have attempted.[/43] I work in digital marketing so the irony is not lost on me. The first few weeks felt genuinely uncomfortable. [50]After about three months, however, I noticed that my concentration had improved dramatically and I was reading again for the first time in years - not just scrolling, but actually sitting with a book for an hour without distraction.[/50] I now protect that time fiercely.
texts.B:
### Patrick, 24, Computer Science Graduate
[46]I wrote my dissertation on the attention economy and was completely convinced by the argument that addictive design is intentional - and then discovered I was just as incapable as anyone else of simply putting my phone down.[/46] Knowing the mechanism does not make you immune to it. [49]My employer expects immediate responses to messages at all hours, and there is an unspoken cultural norm that being available equals being committed - questioning that expectation feels professionally risky.[/49] [52]Individual willpower is necessary but not sufficient - the real change will require design regulation, employer policy reform, and possibly legislation to restrict the most manipulative features of these platforms.[/52]
texts.C:
### Vivienne, 50, Child Psychologist
[45]In my clinical practice I see adolescents whose capacity for sustained attention has been significantly shortened, and I believe the relentless stimulation of multiple simultaneous screens is a key factor - not the only one, but too important to ignore.[/45] This is not technophobia; it is a genuine clinical observation. [48]What I find striking is that when I discuss this with parents, they are often resistant to examining their own patterns - if you check your phone at the dinner table, you are modelling behaviour regardless of what rules you set for your children.[/48] The conversation about screen time must include adults.
texts.D:
### Fraser, 61, Retired School Principal
[44]When I stopped checking emails and news apps in the evening, the change in my relationship with my wife was immediate - we started having actual conversations again, rather than sitting in silence in the same room, each absorbed in our own screen.[/44] It sounds trivial but the cumulative effect of that habit was significant. [47]I have taken up woodworking in the evenings, which requires complete focus and has no digital component whatsoever - the satisfaction of making something physical with your hands is a powerful antidote to a day spent in the abstract world of information.[/47] [51]My grandchildren have never known a world without smartphones, and I find myself wondering what habits they will need to develop that my generation never had to think about.[/51]

questions.question: describes making a deliberate personal decision to limit their use of their phone?
questions.explanation: Lena made a conscious decision to stop checking her phone for the first hour of every morning and the last hour before bed.

questions.question: explains how reducing screen time had a positive effect on a personal relationship?
questions.explanation: Fraser says that when he stopped checking emails in the evening, the change in his relationship with his wife was immediate - they started having actual conversations again.

questions.question: raises concerns about the effect of screen use on the young people they work with?
questions.explanation: Vivienne sees adolescents in her clinical practice whose capacity for sustained attention has been significantly shortened by screen stimulation.

questions.question: found that their professional understanding of the problem did not help them solve it personally?
questions.explanation: Patrick wrote his dissertation on addictive design and was convinced by the argument, yet discovered he was just as incapable of putting his phone down.

questions.question: began a new offline activity to replace time previously spent on technology?
questions.explanation: Fraser has taken up woodworking in the evenings, which requires complete focus and has no digital component.

questions.question: points out that adults are not always consistent in the standards they set for others?
questions.explanation: Vivienne notes that parents are often resistant to examining their own phone habits, even when setting rules for their children's screen time.

questions.question: notes that workplace culture makes it difficult to disconnect from technology?
questions.explanation: Patrick says his employer expects immediate responses at all hours and there is an unspoken norm that being available equals being committed.

questions.question: describes a specific improvement in their daily life after reducing screen time?
questions.explanation: Lena noticed her concentration had improved dramatically and she was reading for extended periods again for the first time in years.

questions.question: reflects on how their generation and today's young people have very different relationships with technology?
questions.explanation: Fraser reflects that his grandchildren have never known a world without smartphones and wonders what habits they will need that his generation never had to develop.

questions.question: argues that addressing excessive screen use requires changes at a broader level, not just individual action?
questions.explanation: Patrick argues that individual willpower is not sufficient and that real change requires design regulation, employer policy reform, and possibly legislation.
